summary: SeedProd Coming Soon/Maintenance Mode plugin (1M+ installs). Despite a priority score of 393 with flagged taint flows, manual review found no exploitable unauthenticated attack vectors. The single nopriv AJAX handler has strong OTH protection. The API-based mode toggle requires knowledge of a server-side secret. All flagged taint flows from the automated scan involve admin-context data (get_option values sent to first-party API endpoints) that require authenticated admin access to manipulate.

findings.title: Missing Nonce and Capability Check in seedprod_lite_template_subscribe
findings.description: The wp_ajax_seedprod_lite_template_subscribe handler lacks both nonce verification and capability checks. Any authenticated WordPress user (subscriber+) can trigger it via a simple AJAX POST.
findings.impact: Minimal. The function only calls update_option('seedprod_free_templates_subscribed', true), which enables free template access in the plugin UI. No data exfiltration, no privilege escalation, no state modification of consequence.
findings.proof: POST to /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=seedprod_lite_template_subscribe as any authenticated user. No nonce or capability check blocks the call.

findings.title: Unauthenticated Coming Soon/Maintenance Mode Toggle via API Key
findings.description: The csp_mm_api() method in render-csp-mm.php allows unauthenticated toggling of Coming Soon or Maintenance Mode by passing seedprod_token and seedprod_mode as URL parameters. The token is compared to the seedprod_api_key option or the SEEDPROD_API_KEY constant. No nonce is used; no rate limiting exists.
findings.impact: If the API key is leaked or guessed, an attacker can enable/disable Coming Soon or Maintenance Mode for the entire site. This could be used for denial of service (enable maintenance mode) or to disable a coming soon page prematurely. The API key is typically a UUID-style token, making brute force impractical.
findings.proof: curl 'https://target.com/?seedprod_token=<API_KEY>&seedprod_mode=2' toggles maintenance mode without authentication.
findings.remediation: Add rate limiting. Consider requiring authentication or using a proper nonce-based approach instead of a static API key.

findings.title: Nopriv One-Click Upgrade Handler - Well Protected
findings.description: wp_ajax_nopriv_seedprod_lite_run_one_click_upgrade is accessible without authentication, but is protected by a one-time hash (OTH) verification using HMAC-SHA512 with wp_salt(). The OTH is generated server-side during the authenticated upgrade flow and deleted after single use, preventing replay. The download URL comes from the SeedProd API based on the license key, not from user input.
findings.impact: None. The OTH mechanism effectively prevents unauthorized use. An attacker would need to know both the OTH value and call it before the legitimate upgrade completes.
findings.remediation: No action needed. The OTH mechanism is adequate.

findings.title: Stored CSS/JS Injection in Preview Template (Admin-Only Write)
findings.description: seedprod-preview.php outputs header_scripts, body_scripts, footer_scripts, headCss, and customCss from page settings (post_content_filtered) without escaping. This is intentional for a page builder - these fields allow admins to inject custom CSS/JS. The content is stored in the database and rendered to all visitors (including unauthenticated users viewing the coming soon page).
findings.impact: By design. Only users with edit_others_posts capability can modify these values through the builder. This is standard page builder functionality. If an admin account is compromised, an attacker could inject malicious scripts, but that's true of any page builder or WordPress admin access generally.
findings.remediation: No action needed. This is expected behavior for a page builder plugin.

findings.title: Taint Flows to SeedProd API (SSRF-001 through SSRF-004)
findings.description: Multiple taint flows flagged in functions-openai.php where get_option values (seedprod_token, seedprod_api_token, seedprod_api_key) are sent to SeedProd's first-party API via wp_remote_post(). The URL is hardcoded (SEEDPROD_API_URL/SEEDPROD_WEB_API_URL), not user-controlled. The option values are only used as authentication tokens in the request body/headers, not as URLs.
findings.impact: None. The URLs are hardcoded to first-party endpoints. The option values are authentication tokens sent in the request body, not URL parameters that could redirect the request. All handlers require authentication and nonce verification.
findings.remediation: No action needed. The automated scanner over-flagged get_option() as a taint source.

findings.title: extract() Usage in Legacy Backwards Compatibility Code
findings.description: extract() is used in render-csp-mm.php line 38 (extracts seedprod_settings from JSON) and in csp4-functions.php lines 9 and 105 (extracts settings from get_option). While extract() can lead to variable injection, in this case the data comes from get_option (database) not from user input directly.
findings.impact: Low. The extracted data comes from database options, not direct user input. An attacker would need admin access to modify the option values. The main risk is code maintainability and potential future bugs if the option structure changes.
findings.remediation: Replace extract() with explicit variable assignments.

taint_flow_disposition.notes: All 7 critical taint flows from the automated scan were false positives or informational. The SSRF flows involve hardcoded first-party API URLs with option-stored tokens as request body data. The XSS flows involve admin-stored page builder content rendered by design. The actual confirmed issues (CS-001, CS-002) were found through manual code review, not from the automated taint analysis.
